eyebrow: Architecture Palette · Sourced from material & light
tagline: Forty-eight buildings, read for their material, not their blueprint.
metaNote:
Color sourced from material,
surface & regional light
sourcing: Every palette is sampled from a specific building or movement's actual materials, surfaces, and the regional light that falls on them — concrete, tile, brick, stone, timber, and metal as they really weather and glow. Iconic works are named; the aim is the colour you would touch, not a generic 'modern' grey.

volumes.title: Brutalism
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol I · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from raw concrete, board-marked and weathered.
preface: Brutalism is a palette of béton brut — raw concrete — in its many weathers: fresh grey, rain-streaked, board-marked, and warmed by the timber and carpet of its interiors. The colour is monolithic, tonal, and textural.
Each refuses the cold dead-grey caricature: real brutalist concrete is warm, varied, and full of the grain of its formwork.

palettes.kicker: The Barbican Estate · 1976 · Chamberlin, Powell & Bon · London
palettes.title: Board-marked concrete towers over planted lakes and warm interior wood
palettes.source: Color from the estate: board-marked concrete grey, pick-hammered warm grey, lakeside green, interior teak, and a brick-red tile.

d.text: Board-marked warm-grey concrete is the estate's body.

s.text: Weathered grey, planting green, and teak.

a.text: Brick tile and slate water as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat dead grey. The Barbican's concrete is warm, hand-textured, and planted — never the cold slab of the cliché.

palettes.kicker: Boston City Hall · 1968 · Kallmann McKinnell & Knowles
palettes.title: An inverted-ziggurat of heavy concrete over a vast brick plaza
palettes.source: Color from the civic monument: heavy concrete grey, red-brick plaza, shadow-recess black, weather-stain grey-brown, and a flag red.

swatches.name: Concrete, heavy grey
swatches.note: The massive precast and poured structure.

swatches.name: Plaza brick, red
swatches.note: The nine acres of brick paving.

swatches.name: Sky, pale blue
swatches.note: The open New England sky above.

d.text: Heavy concrete grey is the building's mass.

s.text: Brick plaza, black recess, and weather stain.

a.text: Flag red and pale sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Uniform light grey. The building reads in deep shadow and warm brick; flat grey loses its monumental contrast.

palettes.kicker: Habitat 67 · 1967 · Moshe Safdie · Montreal
palettes.title: Stacked concrete housing modules over the St Lawrence, each with a garden terrace
palettes.source: Color from the modular block: precast concrete pale grey, terrace-garden green, window-reveal white, river blue, and a balcony red.

d.text: Pale precast grey is the stacked modules' body.

s.text: Terrace green, white reveal, and river blue.

a.text: Signal red and shadow-gap grey as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Monotone grey block. Habitat is grey concrete softened by garden green and bright accents — a lived-in stack, not a slab.

palettes.kicker: Trellick Tower · 1972 · Ernő Goldfinger · London
palettes.title: A slender brutalist housing tower with a detached service shaft and coloured panels
palettes.source: Color from the tower: board-marked concrete grey, balcony-panel red, glazed-shaft grey, sky blue, and a teal infill.

swatches.name: Sky, pale blue
swatches.note: The London sky behind the slab.

d.text: Board-marked concrete grey is the tower's body.

s.text: Shaft grey, balcony glass, and pale sky.

a.text: Signal-red and teal panels as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Dead grey slab. Trellick's concrete carries red and teal panels — varied and inhabited, not a monolithic grey wall.

volumes.title: Art Deco
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol II · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the Jazz-Age skyline, chrome, terracotta, and pastel.
preface: Art Deco runs from Manhattan's chrome-and-stone towers to the pastel stucco of Miami Beach — geometric, luxurious, and colour-confident, with metal, glazed terracotta, and bold trim. The palettes are glamorous and varied by region.
Each refuses the beige 'old building' assumption: Deco is chrome, jade, coral, and gold by design.

palettes.kicker: The Chrysler Building · 1930 · William Van Alen · New York
palettes.title: A stainless-steel crown gleaming over grey-brick setbacks against the sky
palettes.source: Color from the tower: stainless steel silver, grey-brick shaft, dark-stone trim, lobby red-marble, and a gilt accent.

swatches.name: Sky, clear blue
swatches.note: The sky the crown cuts into.

d.text: Warm grey brick is the tower's shaft.

s.text: Steel silver, dark trim, and red marble.

a.text: Gilt gold and clear sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Sepia vintage tower. The Chrysler is bright steel, grey brick, and red marble — gleaming, not a faded old photo.

palettes.kicker: Ocean Drive · 1930s · Miami Beach Art Deco Historic District
palettes.title: A strip of pastel deco hotels in flamingo pink, mint, and Caribbean turquoise
palettes.source: Color from South Beach: stucco flamingo pink, mint green, sky turquoise, cream trim, and a neon coral.

d.text: Flamingo-pink stucco leads the strip.

s.text: Mint trim, sky turquoise, and cream stucco.

a.text: Coral neon and Caribbean sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Muted heritage colour. South Beach is candy pastel by design — pink, mint, and turquoise, not a tasteful neutral.

palettes.kicker: Napier · rebuilt 1931–33 · Art Deco town, New Zealand
palettes.title: A small seaside town rebuilt after earthquake in soft deco ochre, jade, and rose
palettes.source: Color from rebuilt Napier: stucco ochre-cream, deco jade-green, soft rose, sunburst gold trim, and a Pacific blue.

d.text: Ochre-cream stucco is the town's field.

s.text: Jade trim, soft rose, and warm shadow.

a.text: Gold sunburst and sea blue as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Plain whitewash. Napier is a coordinated pastel deco ensemble — ochre, jade, and rose, not a uniform white town.

palettes.kicker: Marine Drive · 1930s · Art Deco ensemble · Mumbai
palettes.title: A curving seafront of cream-and-pastel deco apartment blocks above the Arabian Sea
palettes.source: Color from the Queen's Necklace: cream stucco, deco mint and butter trim, sea-blue, palm green, and a sunset coral.

d.text: Cream stucco leads the seafront ensemble.

s.text: Butter and mint trim with sea blue.

a.text: Coral sunset and palm green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Drab concrete grey. Marine Drive is a coordinated cream-and-pastel deco curve, weathered but still colour-confident.

volumes.title: Gothic Cathedral
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol III · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the great churches, stone, glass, and coloured light.
preface: The Gothic cathedral is grey stone transformed by coloured light — the deep blues and reds of stained glass thrown across pale limestone, candle gold, and the dark of the vaults. The palette is cool stone lit by jewelled fire.
Each refuses the flat 'grey church' assumption: the cathedral's whole purpose is stone dissolved in coloured light.

palettes.kicker: Chartres Cathedral · c.1220 · the nave at midday
palettes.title: Pale limestone columns washed by the deep blue and red of the great windows
palettes.source: Color from Chartres: pale limestone grey, 'Chartres blue' glass, ruby-glass red, candle gold, and vault shadow.

swatches.name: Ruby glass, deep red
swatches.note: The red of the great rose.

swatches.name: Glass green, emerald
swatches.note: The green pot-metal in the windows.

d.text: Pale limestone is the cathedral's body.

s.text: Chartres blue, ruby red, and vault shadow.

a.text: Candle gold and emerald glass as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat stone grey. Chartres is grey stone drenched in cobalt and ruby light — the colour is the architecture.

palettes.kicker: Sainte-Chapelle · 1248 · Paris · the upper chapel
palettes.title: A jewel-box chapel where the walls dissolve into towering panels of stained glass
palettes.source: Color from the glass cage: saturated cobalt blue, ruby red, gilt-rib gold, leaf-green glass, and a violet.

swatches.name: Window cobalt, deep blue
swatches.note: The dominant blue of the 15 windows.

swatches.name: Ruby glass, red
swatches.note: The blazing reds beside the blue.

d.text: Deep cobalt blue dominates the glass walls.

s.text: Ruby red, gilt rib, and leaf green.

a.text: Glass violet and pale stone as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Muted heritage palette. Sainte-Chapelle is near-total saturated glass — blue and red fire, with stone almost gone.

palettes.kicker: Sagrada Família · Gaudí, begun 1882 · Barcelona · the nave
palettes.title: A forest of branching stone columns washed in the blue, green, and orange of modern glass
palettes.source: Color from Gaudí's nave: warm stone cream, sunrise-side blue-green glass, sunset-side orange-red glass, canopy green, and gold.

d.text: Warm cream stone is the column forest.

s.text: Blue-green dawn, orange-red dusk, and canopy green.

a.text: Vault gold and glass violet as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Cold grey modern church. Gaudí floods warm stone with sunrise-blue and sunset-orange — a coloured forest, not a grey hall.

palettes.kicker: Cologne Cathedral · 1880 (completed) · the dark nave
palettes.title: Soot-darkened limestone piers soaring into shadow lit by deep medieval glass
palettes.source: Color from Cologne: soot-dark stone, deep-glass red and blue, candle gold, vault black, and a grey daylight.

swatches.name: Glass, deep blue
swatches.note: The blue of the old windows.

d.text: Soot-dark stone is the towering nave.

s.text: Deep blue and red glass and black vault.

a.text: Candle gold and pale daylight as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Bright clean limestone. Cologne's stone is soot-darkened to grey-brown; its colour is deep glass against shadow.

volumes.title: Japanese Traditional
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol IV · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the Japanese house, wood, paper, and tatami.
preface: Traditional Japanese architecture is a palette of natural materials in restraint: cedar and cypress wood, paper screens, tatami straw, clay plaster, and the dark of lacquer and tile. The colour is warm, muted, and tuned to filtered light.
Each refuses the red-lacquer-temple cliché: most Japanese building is quiet wood, paper, and earth, with red reserved for shrines.

palettes.kicker: Katsura Imperial Villa · 17th c · Kyoto
palettes.title: A refined garden villa of unpainted cedar posts, paper shoji, and tatami
palettes.source: Color from Katsura: unpainted cedar post, shoji-paper cream, tatami green-straw, clay-plaster grey, and a black-lacquer trim.

swatches.name: Garden moss, green
swatches.note: The moss seen through the opening.

d.text: Warm cedar is the villa's structural field.

s.text: Cream shoji, green tatami, and clay plaster.

a.text: Black lacquer and garden moss as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Red-lacquer temple colour. Katsura is restrained natural wood, paper, and straw — refined understatement, not shrine red.

palettes.kicker: Ise Grand Shrine · rebuilt every 20 years · Mie Prefecture
palettes.title: A Shinto shrine of fresh blond hinoki cypress and gold-capped thatch
palettes.source: Color from Ise: fresh blond hinoki cypress, thatch gold, gilt-cap brass, gravel grey-white, and forest green.

swatches.name: Sacred forest, deep green
swatches.note: The cryptomeria grove around the shrine.

swatches.name: Shadow eave, brown
swatches.note: The dark under the deep eaves.

d.text: Blond hinoki cypress is the shrine's body.

s.text: Gold thatch, grey gravel, and forest green.

a.text: Brass gilt and eave shadow as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Aged dark-temple brown. Ise is rebuilt new in pale blond cypress — fresh, light wood, not weathered antiquity.

palettes.kicker: Kyō-machiya townhouse · Edo–Meiji · Kyoto
palettes.title: A narrow merchant townhouse of dark lattice, ochre plaster, and indigo noren
palettes.source: Color from the machiya: dark-cedar lattice, ochre-clay wall, indigo noren curtain, tatami straw, and a copper-green gutter.

d.text: Dark cedar lattice defines the street front.

s.text: Ochre plaster, indigo noren, and straw cream.

a.text: Charcoal tile and verdigris copper as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Bright tourist colour. The machiya is dark wood, earth ochre, and indigo — sober merchant restraint, not festival brights.

palettes.kicker: Himeji Castle · 1609 · 'White Heron' keep · Japan
palettes.title: A brilliant white-plaster castle keep with grey tile roofs and gilt crests
palettes.source: Color from the White Heron: lime-plaster white, grey-tile roof, dark-timber gable, gilt shachihoko, and pine green.

d.text: Lime-white plaster is the keep's brilliant body.

s.text: Grey tile, dark gable, and stone base.

a.text: Gilt ornament and pine green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Dark aged timber. Himeji is brilliant white plaster with grey roofs — the 'White Heron', not a brown wooden fortress.

volumes.title: Mediterranean Vernacular
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol V · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the sun-built south, lime, earth, and shade.
preface: Mediterranean vernacular building uses local lime, earth, and stone against hard sun — whitewash, terracotta, ochre, and the deep blue of shutter and dome. The palette is bright, warm, and made for heat.
Each refuses the single 'Santorini blue-and-white' shorthand: each region has its own earth, lime, and shutter colour.

palettes.kicker: Oia · Cyclades vernacular · Santorini, Greece
palettes.title: Whitewashed cave-houses and blue domes stepping down a volcanic caldera
palettes.source: Color from the Cyclades: lime whitewash, dome blue, volcanic-rock grey-red, Aegean blue, and a bougainvillea magenta.

swatches.name: Bougainvillea, magenta
swatches.note: The vine over the white walls.

d.text: Lime whitewash is the village's dominant field.

s.text: Dome blue, volcanic rock, and sea blue.

a.text: Bougainvillea magenta and blue shade as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat blue-and-white only. Santorini also has volcanic red-grey rock and magenta vine; pure blue-white is a fridge magnet.

palettes.kicker: Andalusian patio · Moorish-Spanish vernacular · Córdoba
palettes.title: A cool tiled courtyard of whitewash, azulejo blue, geranium red, and orange tree
palettes.source: Color from the patio: lime whitewash, azulejo blue-and-yellow tile, geranium red, orange-tree green, and terracotta pot.

swatches.name: Azulejo, cobalt blue
swatches.note: The glazed wall and floor tiles.

swatches.name: Tile yellow, ochre
swatches.note: The yellow in the azulejo pattern.

d.text: Lime whitewash is the patio's cool field.

s.text: Cobalt azulejo, orange-tree green, and clay pots.

a.text: Geranium red and tile ochre as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Plain rustic neutral. The Andalusian patio is whitewash set with cobalt tile and red geranium — cool colour against heat.

palettes.kicker: Riad · Moroccan vernacular · Marrakech
palettes.title: An inward courtyard house of rose-clay walls, zellige tile, and carved cedar
palettes.source: Color from the riad: Marrakech rose-clay, zellige green-and-blue tile, carved-cedar brown, brass lantern, and a saffron textile.

swatches.name: Carved cedar, brown
swatches.note: The fretted wood screens and doors.

d.text: Rose clay is the riad's earthen field.

s.text: Emerald zellige, carved cedar, and cream plaster.

a.text: Brass lantern and saffron textile as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Sandy-beige monotone. The riad is rose earth set with emerald tile and saffron cloth — rich colour inside the walls.

palettes.kicker: Trulli of Alberobello · vernacular · Puglia, Italy
palettes.title: Whitewashed conical stone houses with grey limestone roofs and painted symbols
palettes.source: Color from the trulli: lime whitewash, grey limestone-cone roof, painted-symbol white, olive green, and a Puglian sky blue.

d.text: Lime whitewash is the trulli's dominant wall.

s.text: Grey-stone cones, olive green, and red earth.

a.text: Painted symbols and sky blue as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Rustic brown-stone. The trulli are bright whitewash under grey cones against red earth — a crisp two-tone, not a muddy vernacular.

volumes.title: Modernism & Bauhaus
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol VI · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the modern movement, white walls and primary accents.
preface: Modernism stripped ornament to white render, glass, and steel — with the Bauhaus and De Stijl adding disciplined primary accents. The palette is clean, light, and precise, with colour used as deliberate punctuation.
Each refuses the all-white sterility: modernism uses white as a ground for primary red, blue, and yellow, plus natural stone and wood.

palettes.kicker: Villa Savoye · 1931 · Le Corbusier · Poissy
palettes.title: A white box on pilotis floating over green lawn, with soft interior pastels
palettes.source: Color from the villa: render white, lawn green, pilotis-shadow grey, interior pale-blue and rose, and a ramp black.

swatches.name: Lawn, green
swatches.note: The green field it floats over.

d.text: White render is the villa's dominant body.

s.text: Lawn green, pilotis grey, and pale-blue interior.

a.text: Rose accent and black ramp as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Pure clinical white. Savoye sets white against green lawn and soft interior pastels — not a single sterile field.

palettes.kicker: Barcelona Pavilion · 1929 · Mies van der Rohe
palettes.title: A flowing plane of travertine, green marble, and onyx under a flat roof
palettes.source: Color from the pavilion: travertine cream, green Tinian marble, golden onyx, chrome-column silver, and a deep pool grey.

swatches.name: Travertine, cream
swatches.note: The pale stone floor and walls.

d.text: Cream travertine is the pavilion's ground.

s.text: Green marble, golden onyx, and chrome.

a.text: Dark pool and ruby drape as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat grey minimalism. Mies's pavilion is rich stone — green marble and golden onyx — luxury in restraint, not bare grey.

palettes.kicker: Bauhaus Dessau · 1926 · Walter Gropius
palettes.title: A glass-curtain-wall workshop block with white render and primary signage
palettes.source: Color from the school: white render, glass-curtain grey-green, primary red door, steel-grey frame, and a black-lettered sign.

d.text: White render is the school's dominant surface.

s.text: Glass-wall grey-green, steel frame, and pale soffit.

a.text: Primary red and black signage as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Colourless functionalism. The Bauhaus uses white and glass as a ground for sharp primary-red accents and bold type.

palettes.kicker: Farnsworth House · 1951 · Mies van der Rohe · Illinois
palettes.title: A white-steel-and-glass pavilion floating above a floodplain meadow by the river
palettes.source: Color from the glass house: white-steel frame, glass reflection grey, meadow green, travertine deck cream, and a river-maple amber.

swatches.name: Glass wall, reflective grey-green
swatches.note: The full-height glazing mirroring the trees.

swatches.name: Meadow, green
swatches.note: The floodplain grass it hovers over.

swatches.name: River maple, autumn amber
swatches.note: The black maples by the Fox River.

d.text: White steel is the pavilion's defining frame.

s.text: Reflective glass, meadow green, and travertine.

a.text: Maple amber and cool shadow as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Cold grey box. Farnsworth's white steel frames a green meadow seen through glass — nature is part of the palette.

volumes.title: Islamic Architecture
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol VII · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the Islamic world, tile, light, and geometry.
preface: Islamic architecture turns surface into colour — glazed tile in turquoise and cobalt, carved stucco, honey stone, and the play of filtered light through screens. The palettes are luminous, geometric, and tile-rich.
Each refuses a flat 'desert sand' assumption: the great monuments are jewelled with turquoise, cobalt, and gold.

palettes.kicker: The Alhambra · 14th c · Granada · the Court of the Lions
palettes.title: Carved-stucco honey walls, slender columns, and azulejo dados around a fountain
palettes.source: Color from the Nasrid palace: honey stucco, azulejo blue-green tile, terracotta, ceiling cedar, and a gilt muqarnas.

d.text: Honey stucco is the palace's carved field.

s.text: Blue-green azulejo, terracotta, and cedar.

a.text: Gilt muqarnas and myrtle green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat sandstone beige. The Alhambra glows honey-stucco set with blue-green tile and gold — colour and light, not bare stone.

palettes.kicker: Shah Mosque · 1629 · Isfahan, Iran · the dome and iwan
palettes.title: A vast turquoise dome and cobalt-tiled iwan covered in floral arabesque
palettes.source: Color from Isfahan: dome turquoise, cobalt-tile blue, tile-yellow, white arabesque, and a brick-buff base.

swatches.name: Cobalt tile, deep blue
swatches.note: The darker blue of the patterns.

swatches.name: Arabesque white, cream
swatches.note: The white tracery in the tile.

swatches.name: Tile green, leaf
swatches.note: The green stems of the floral work.

d.text: Turquoise dome tile leads the palette.

s.text: Cobalt blue, gold yellow, and buff brick.

a.text: White arabesque and leaf green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Monochrome stone. Isfahan is total turquoise-and-cobalt tile with gold floral — a jewelled surface, not bare masonry.

palettes.kicker: The Taj Mahal · 1648 · Agra · at dawn
palettes.title: A white-marble mausoleum shifting from rose to gold to white over its reflecting pool
palettes.source: Color from the Taj at dawn: white marble blushing rose, pietra-dura inlay colour, red-sandstone gateway, pool blue, and garden green.

swatches.name: Marble, dawn-rose white
swatches.note: The white marble tinted by sunrise.

swatches.name: Inlay, lapis blue
swatches.note: The blue stones of the inlay.

d.text: Dawn-rose marble is the mausoleum's body.

s.text: Red sandstone, pool blue, and cypress green.

a.text: Jasper-red and lapis-blue inlay as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat brilliant white. The Taj blushes rose and gold with the sun and is set with red sandstone — colour, not pure white.

palettes.kicker: Mezquita of Córdoba · 8th–10th c · the hypostyle hall
palettes.title: A forest of columns under red-and-white double arches in candlelit shadow
palettes.source: Color from the Great Mosque: arch red-and-white voussoirs, jasper-column grey, mihrab gold-mosaic, shadow brown, and a tile green.

swatches.name: Arch voussoir, terracotta red
swatches.note: The alternating red brick of the arches.

swatches.name: Arch voussoir, pale stone
swatches.note: The white stone alternating with red.

swatches.name: Hall shadow, brown
swatches.note: The dim depth of the column forest.

swatches.name: Mihrab, gold mosaic
swatches.note: The Byzantine gold of the prayer niche.

swatches.name: Mosaic, emerald green
swatches.note: The green smalti of the mihrab.

d.text: Terracotta-red arch brick leads the hall.

s.text: Pale-stone voussoirs, grey columns, and brown shadow.

a.text: Gold and emerald mihrab mosaic as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Monochrome stone. The Mezquita's signature is the red-and-white striped arch over grey columns — rhythmic colour, not bare masonry.

volumes.title: Scandinavian & Nordic
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol VIII · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the north, pale wood, falu red, and soft light.
preface: Nordic architecture works with pale birch and pine, white plaster, the deep falu-red of farm timber, and turf, all under low northern light. The palette is warm-natural and quiet, tuned to long winters.
Each refuses cold minimalist grey: the Nordic palette is warm pale wood and falu red, not a sterile white box.

palettes.kicker: Säynätsalo Town Hall · 1952 · Alvar Aalto · Finland
palettes.title: A red-brick civic block around a raised grass courtyard with teak and copper
palettes.source: Color from Aalto: warm red brick, raised-courtyard grass green, teak interior, copper-green roof, and pine-batten ceiling.

d.text: Warm red brick is the building's body.

s.text: Courtyard grass, teak, and blond pine.

a.text: Verdigris copper and brass as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Cold white minimalism. Aalto's town hall is warm brick, teak, and pine — humane natural material, not bare white.

palettes.kicker: Borgund Stave Church · c.1180 · Norway
palettes.title: A black tar-pine stave church with dragon-head gables against a green fjord valley
palettes.source: Color from the stave church: tar-black pine, shingle grey-brown, fjord-valley green, mountain-grey, and a dragon-head silhouette.

swatches.name: Tarred pine, black-brown
swatches.note: The pine staves coated in protective tar.

swatches.name: Fjord valley, green
swatches.note: The grass and forest around it.

d.text: Tar-black pine is the church's dominant body.

s.text: Grey-brown shingle, valley green, and mountain grey.

a.text: Carved portal and pale sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Warm honey-wood cabin. The stave church is tar-black against green — dark, ancient timber, not a bright log cabin.

palettes.kicker: Falu-red farmstead · Swedish vernacular · Dalarna
palettes.title: A red-painted timber farm with white trim and a turf-and-birch landscape
palettes.source: Color from the farm: falu-red timber, white window-trim, birch-trunk pale, meadow green, and a grey shingle roof.

swatches.name: Window trim, white
swatches.note: The painted corner and window boards.

d.text: Falu red is the farmstead's dominant timber.

s.text: White trim, pale birch, and meadow green.

a.text: Grey shingle and ochre door as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Muted grey-wood. The Swedish farm is deep falu-red with crisp white trim — a confident colour pairing, not bare timber.

palettes.kicker: Icelandic turf house · vernacular · Skógar / Glaumbær
palettes.title: Grass-roofed turf farmhouses set into the green, with white-and-black timber gables
palettes.source: Color from the turf house: roof-turf green, dark-timber gable, white window-trim, basalt-stone grey, and a heath-purple.

swatches.name: Turf roof, grass green
swatches.note: The growing grass roof and walls.

d.text: Grass-green turf is the house's living skin.

s.text: Dark gable, white trim, and basalt grey.

a.text: Heath purple and pale sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Brown mud-hut cliché. The turf house is vivid grass-green with crisp white-trimmed dark gables — alive, not a dirt hovel.

volumes.title: American Vernacular
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol IX · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the American grain, adobe, clapboard, and brick.
preface: American vernacular building is regional and material-led: Southwestern adobe, New England clapboard, prairie brick-and-oak, and the painted ladies of the West Coast. The palette shifts with local earth and weather.
Each refuses a single 'farmhouse' look: each region's vernacular has its own earth, paint, and timber colour.

palettes.kicker: Taos Pueblo · adobe vernacular · New Mexico
palettes.title: Stacked adobe dwellings the colour of the earth, with turquoise doors and viga beams
palettes.source: Color from the pueblo: adobe earth tan, turquoise door, viga-beam brown, sky blue, and a chile-ristra red.

swatches.name: Adobe, earth tan
swatches.note: The mud-plaster walls, the colour of the ground.

swatches.name: Door, turquoise
swatches.note: The protective blue-green doors and frames.

swatches.name: Desert sky, blue
swatches.note: The high clear New Mexico sky.

d.text: Adobe earth-tan is the pueblo's body.

s.text: Turquoise doors, viga brown, and sky blue.

a.text: Chile red and piñon green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat beige stucco. Taos adobe is warm earth set with turquoise doors and red chile — the colour of place, not a builder beige.

palettes.kicker: New England saltbox · colonial vernacular · coastal Massachusetts
palettes.title: A weathered clapboard farmhouse in barn red or grey shingle by a stone wall
palettes.source: Color from the saltbox: weathered-grey shingle, barn-red clapboard, white trim, autumn-maple, and a granite wall grey.

swatches.name: Trim, white
swatches.note: The painted window and door trim.

d.text: Weathered-grey shingle is the house's body.

s.text: Barn red, white trim, and granite grey.

a.text: Maple scarlet and bottle-green shutter as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Bright modern paint. The saltbox is weathered grey and oxide red with white trim — aged colonial colour, not fresh suburbia.

palettes.kicker: Painted Ladies · Victorian vernacular · San Francisco
palettes.title: A row of Queen-Anne townhouses in multi-colour heritage paint against blue sky
palettes.source: Color from the row: facade sage green, trim cream, accent plum, sky blue, and a gilt detail.

d.text: Sage green leads a typical painted facade.

s.text: Cream trim, plum accent, and rose neighbour.

a.text: Gilt detail and clear sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Single drab colour. The Painted Ladies use three-to-five coordinated heritage colours per house — polychrome by design.

palettes.kicker: Charleston single house · antebellum vernacular · South Carolina
palettes.title: A pastel stucco house turned side-on with deep tiered piazzas in the Lowcountry heat
palettes.source: Color from the single house: Charleston stucco pastel (haint-blue ceiling), piazza-shade green, shutter dark green, brick pink, and white trim.

swatches.name: Piazza ceiling, haint blue
swatches.note: The porch ceiling painted to ward spirits.

d.text: Pastel stucco leads the side-on facade.

s.text: Haint-blue ceiling, green shutter, and pink brick.

a.text: White trim and mossy oak as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Plain white colonial. The Charleston single house is soft pastel stucco with a haint-blue porch ceiling — coastal colour, not stark white.

volumes.title: Constructivist & Soviet
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol X · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the revolutionary state, concrete, red, and marble.
preface: Soviet and Constructivist architecture runs from the bold red-and-grey of the avant-garde to the marble-and-bronze opulence of the Moscow Metro and the raw concrete of late monuments. The palette is industrial, with propaganda red and gilt.
Each refuses a flat 'grey communist' look: from metro marble to mosaic, this is a surprisingly rich and varied tradition.

palettes.kicker: Narkomfin Building · 1930 · Ginzburg · Moscow
palettes.title: A constructivist housing block of grey render, ribbon windows, and primary accents
palettes.source: Color from the constructivist block: grey render, ribbon-window grey-green, pilotis shadow, primary-red door, and a pale interior.

d.text: Concrete-grey render is the block's body.

s.text: Grey-green glass, pilotis shadow, and pale interior.

a.text: Primary red and steel grey as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Drab uniform grey. Constructivism sets grey render against sharp primary-red accents — bold, not monotonous.

palettes.kicker: Komsomolskaya Station · 1952 · Moscow Metro
palettes.title: A baroque-Soviet metro hall of yellow vaults, gilt, and mosaic against marble
palettes.source: Color from the metro palace: vault ochre-yellow, gilt-stucco gold, mosaic blue, red marble, and a white-marble column.

d.text: Ochre-yellow vault is the hall's field.

s.text: Gilt stucco, mosaic blue, and white marble.

a.text: Red marble floor and bronze lamp as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Austere grey transit. The metro is a gilded palace — ochre vaults, gold, and mosaic — opulence underground, not grey utility.

palettes.kicker: Buzludzha Monument · 1981 · Bulgaria · the saucer hall
palettes.title: A derelict concrete flying-saucer monument with red-mosaic ribbons inside
palettes.source: Color from the ruin: weathered concrete grey, red-mosaic ribbon, gold-tessera detail, rust-stain, and a cold sky-blue through broken roof.

swatches.name: Mosaic ribbon, communist red
swatches.note: The red band running the inner ring.

d.text: Weathered concrete grey is the ruin's body.

s.text: Red mosaic, rust stain, and cold sky.

a.text: Dull gold tessera and snow as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Heroic clean colour. The monument is now a rusting grey ruin with surviving red-and-gold mosaic — decay, not glory.

palettes.kicker: VDNKh · 1939–54 · exhibition pavilions · Moscow
palettes.title: Gilded fountains and white-and-gold socialist-classical pavilions of plenty
palettes.source: Color from the exhibition: pavilion cream-white, gilt fountain gold, mosaic red-star, fountain-pool blue, and a flag red.

swatches.name: Friendship fountain, gilt gold
swatches.note: The golden statues of the Soviet republics.

d.text: Cream-white pavilions are the exhibition's body.

s.text: Gilt fountain, pool blue, and red mosaic.

a.text: Wheat gold and ruby star as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Grim grey communism. VDNKh is white-and-gold plenty — gilded fountains and ruby stars, a propaganda of abundance, not austerity.

volumes.title: Postmodern & Expressionist
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol XI · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the playful turn, colour, curve, and quotation.
preface: Postmodern and expressionist architecture brought colour and gesture back — pastel classical quotation, titanium curves, and sculptural white. The palettes are bold, witty, and material-distinctive.
Each refuses the sober modernist neutral: these buildings use colour and sheen as statement.

palettes.kicker: The Portland Building · 1982 · Michael Graves
palettes.title: A postmodern block in cream, terracotta, and teal with oversized classical motifs
palettes.source: Color from the building: cream tile, terracotta pier, teal-green band, maroon accent, and a keystone blue.

swatches.name: Facade tile, cream
swatches.note: The pale body of the block.

d.text: Cream tile is the building's body.

s.text: Terracotta piers, teal band, and maroon base.

a.text: Blue keystone and dark window as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Modernist monochrome. Graves brings classical colour back — cream, terracotta, and teal in witty quotation, not grey glass.

palettes.kicker: Guggenheim Bilbao · 1997 · Frank Gehry
palettes.title: Titanium curves catching the light in silver, gold, and river-grey
palettes.source: Color from the museum: titanium silver, gold-cast highlight, limestone cream, Nervión-river grey-green, and a sky blue.

swatches.name: Titanium highlight, warm gold
swatches.note: The metal turning gold in sun.

swatches.name: Sky reflection, blue
swatches.note: The sky caught in the curves.

d.text: Shifting titanium silver is the building's skin.

s.text: Gold highlight, cream stone, and river grey-green.

a.text: Sky blue and dark glass as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat grey metal. The titanium shifts silver-to-gold with the light; a single grey misses the building's whole effect.

palettes.kicker: Sydney Opera House · 1973 · Jørn Utzon
palettes.title: White sail-shells of glossy tile rising over the blue harbour
palettes.source: Color from the shells: tile white-cream, glossy-tile highlight, harbour blue, granite-podium pink, and a sky blue.

swatches.name: Glass wall, bronze-grey
swatches.note: The amber-glass mouth of the shells.

d.text: White-cream shell tile is the building's body.

s.text: Tile highlight, harbour blue, and pink granite.

a.text: Bright sky and bronze glass as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Pure flat white. The shells are subtly two-toned glossy tile over a pink-granite podium against deep harbour blue.

palettes.kicker: Piazza d'Italia · 1978 · Charles Moore · New Orleans
palettes.title: A postmodern public plaza of neon-lit classical columns in terracotta, ochre, and teal
palettes.source: Color from the piazza: terracotta stucco, ochre column, neon-cyan accent, fountain-blue water, and a stainless-steel capital.

d.text: Terracotta stucco is the plaza's ground.

s.text: Ochre columns, teal recess, and fountain blue.

a.text: Electric-cyan neon and steel as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Sober stone classicism. Moore's piazza is pastel-and-neon parody — terracotta, ochre, and cyan, a joke on the temple, not a real one.

volumes.title: Industrial & Warehouse
volumes.eyebrow: Architecture · Vol XII · Sourced from Material & Light
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the working buildings, brick, iron, and glass.
preface: Industrial architecture is brick, cast iron, steel, and big glass — the colour of mills, power stations, and warehouses, weathered by work and soot. The palette is robust, warm-brick or grey-steel, with rust and patina.
Each refuses a sanitised loft-conversion gloss: real industrial colour is sooty brick, oxidised iron, and grimy glass.

palettes.kicker: Lancashire cotton mill · 19th c · northern England
palettes.title: A red-brick spinning mill with iron-framed windows under a soot-grey sky
palettes.source: Color from the mill: soot-stained red brick, cast-iron black, grimy-glass grey, sandstone-sill buff, and a rust-red door.

swatches.name: Mill brick, soot-red
swatches.note: The red brick darkened by industrial soot.

d.text: Soot-red brick is the mill's dominant body.

s.text: Cast-iron black, grimy glass, and buff sill.

a.text: Rusted door and soot sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Clean loft brick. The mill brick is soot-darkened and grimy, framed in black iron — a working surface, not a sandblasted feature wall.

palettes.kicker: Bankside / Tate Modern · 1947, conv. 2000 · London
palettes.title: A vast brick power station with a steel chimney, turbine-hall grey light
palettes.source: Color from the power station: brown-brick mass, steel-window grey, turbine-hall concrete, chimney grey, and a slate-Thames blue.

d.text: Brown brick is the power station's mass.

s.text: Steel-window grey, concrete, and chimney grey.

a.text: Slate Thames and pale light as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Bright gallery white. Bankside keeps its industrial brown brick and grey steel — monumental and sober, not whitewashed.

palettes.kicker: SoHo cast-iron loft · 1880s · New York
palettes.title: A cast-iron commercial facade painted grey-green over big shop windows
palettes.source: Color from the cast-iron front: painted grey-green iron, large-window glass, cornice shadow, fire-escape black, and a brick party-wall red.

d.text: Grey-green painted iron is the facade's body.

s.text: Window glass, cornice shadow, and brick wall.

a.text: Black fire escape and asphalt as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Trendy matte-black loft. The cast-iron front was painted stone-imitating grey-green; the black-and-rust loft look is a later fashion.

palettes.kicker: Zollverein Coal Mine · 1932 · Schupp & Kremmer · Essen, Germany
palettes.title: A Bauhaus-industrial winding tower and coking plant in red steel and rust
palettes.source: Color from the ironworks: steel-red oxide, riveted-steel grey, brick-infill red, rust-brown, and a sky grey.

swatches.name: Brick infill, dull red
swatches.note: The brick panels between the steel.

swatches.name: Coke ash, charcoal
swatches.note: Coal dust and soot on every surface.

swatches.name: Sky, industrial grey
swatches.note: The Ruhr sky behind the towers.

d.text: Red-oxide steel is the works' structural body.

s.text: Riveted grey, dull brick, and rust brown.

a.text: Coke-ash charcoal and grey sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Clean restored industrial. Zollverein is red-oxide steel, soot, and rust — a working ironworks palette, not a polished museum.
